article:
Chunk # 0.
Isolation of functional mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal
muscle without manual homogenization
Vera S Gross1, Heather K Greenberg2, Sergei V Baranov2,†, Greta M Carlson1, Irina G Stavrovskaya2, Alexander V Lazarev1, and Bruce S Kristal2,*
1Pressure BioSciences Inc, 14 Norfolk Ave, South Easton, MA, 02375
2Department of Neurosurgery, Brigham and Women’s Hospital and, Department of Surgery,
Harvard Medical School, 221 Longwood Avenue, LMRC-322, Boston, Massachusetts 02115
Abstract
Isolation of functional and intact mitochondria from solid tissue is crucial for studies that focus on
the elucidation of normal mitochondrial physiology and/or mitochondrial dysfunction in
conditions such as aging, diabetes and cancer There is growing recognition of the importance of
mitochondria as both targets for drug development and as off-target mediators of drug side effects Unfortunately, mitochondrial isolation from tissue is generally carried out using homogenizer-
based methods that require extensive operator experience to obtain reproducible, high-quality
preparations These methods limit dissemination, impede scale-up, and contribute to difficulties in
reproducing experimental results over time and across laboratories Here we describe semi-
automated methods to disrupt tissue, using kidney and muscle mitochondria preparations as
exemplars These methods utilize either a Barocycler, or The PCT Shredder, or both The PCT
Shredder is a mechanical grinder that quickly breaks up tissue without significant risk of over-
homogenization Mitochondria isolated using The PCT Shredder are shown to be comparable to
controls The Barocycler generates controlled pressure pulses that can be adjusted to lyse cells and
release organelles The mitochondria subjected to pressure cycling-mediated tissue disruption are
shown to retain functionality, enabling combinations of The PCT Shredder and Barocycler to be
used to purify mitochondrial preparations Keywords
Mitochondria; Barocycler; Hydrostatic Pressure; Kidney; Muscle; Drug discovery; Toxicology
Introduction
The most physiologically relevant evaluation of mitochondrial function should be performed
in situ  or in vivo  Unfortunately, under such conditions, it is difficult to attribute
observations to primary mitochondrial physiology in the context of secondary effects related
to the complexity of systems such as intact cells, tissues or animals Accordingly, there are

Anal Biochem  2011 November 15; 418(2): 213±223 doi:10.1016/j.ab.2011.07.017 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptmany experiments that can only be conducted on purified mitochondrial preparations Similarly, many of the questions most relevant to drug development can, theoretically, be
effectively and inexpensively brought to scale in isolated preparations as opposed to intact
animals or cell cultures Therefore, in order to better understand mitochondrial function and
the interrelation of mitochondrial dysfunction with various diseases, experiments often need
to focus on functional isolated mitochondria rather than on intact cells, tissues or animals
[1] Several traditional methods for isolation of functional mitochondria are widely used However, these current protocols, which typically rely on Dounce, Potter-Elvehjem or rotor-
stator shearing homogenizers for initial tissue disruption [2–4], suffer from several critical
drawbacks, of which the most pronounced are the demand for highly skilled personnel and
the hands-on nature of the initial tissue homogenization The homogenization procedure is a
crucial step that can significantly impact the quality and yield of the resulting mitochondria For isolation of muscle mitochondria, for example, the most commonly used method is
manual homogenization in a Potter-Elvehjem tissue grinder This physical homogenization
is assumed to occur by two types of liquid shearing; one due to pestle rotation and the other
due to the up-and-down pestle strokes Generally, both forces need to be precisely controlled
to minimize damage to the mitochondria [5] This requires slow pestle strokes and consistent
pestle rotation During this manual homogenization, it is difficult to know when sufficient
tissue disruption has occurred because large fragments of tissue may still be present, even
when significant cell disruption has occurred In addition, if disruption is carried out long
enough for thorough tissue disaggregation, the resulting mitochondria are likely to be
severely damaged due to over-homogenization For this reason, a more hands-off method,
i.e., one that is less reliant on the experience and skill of the user, is expected to yield more
consistent and reproducible results

**Conclusion:**
The article describes semi-automated methods for disrupting tissue to isolate mitochondria from rat kidney and skeletal muscle. The methods utilize either a Barocycler, The PCT Shredder, or both. The PCT Shredder is a mechanical grinder that breaks up tissue without over-homogenization, while the Barocycler generates pressure pulses to lyse cells and release organelles. The article mentions that mitochondria isolated using The PCT Shredder are comparable to controls, and the mitochondria subjected to pressure cycling-mediated tissue disruption retain functionality. summary:
Iteration 4:
Researchers from Pressure BioSciences Inc, including Vera S Gross, Heather K Greenberg, Sergei V Baranov, Greta M Carlson, Irina G Stavrovskaya, Alexander V Lazarev, and Bruce S Kristal, collaborated with the Department of Neurosurgery at Brigham and Women's Hospital and the Department of Surgery at Harvard Medical School. They focused on the isolation of functional and intact mitochondria from solid tissue, specifically kidney and muscle. Traditional methods of tissue homogenization, such as Dounce and Potter-Elvehjem, were compared with rotor-stator shearing homogenizers. Manual homogenization led to damage to mitochondria and inconsistent results. The use of rotor-stator shearing homogenizers, such as the Barocycler and PCT Shredder, ensured thorough tissue disaggregation and reproducible outcomes. The Barocycler generated controlled pressure pulses to lyse cells and release organelles, while the PCT Shredder quickly broke up tissue without over-homogenization. The combination of these methods allowed for the purification of high-quality mitochondrial preparations. This semi-automated approach has the potential to improve drug development and toxicology studies related to mitochondrial function. The PCT Shredder efficiently homogenizes tissue for drug development and purification. It utilizes pressure pulses to disrupt tissue and preserve organelle functionality. This semi-automated method ensures reproducible results and enables the study of primary mitochondrial physiology and secondary effects. The shredder minimizes damage to mitochondria and allows for thorough tissue disaggregation, leading to consistent observations in in situ and in vivo experiments. The use of functional isolated mitochondria in experiments can provide valuable insights into mitochondrial function and its interrelation with various diseases.

article:
Chunk # 1.
Recently, several authors have used nitrogen cavitation for isolation of mitochondria from
cultured cells [6–8] This method is based on sample pressurization (at ~1500 psi) and the
subsequent formation of gas bubbles when the sample is rapidly decompressed While this
method has several drawbacks, including the need to clean the pressure chamber between
each sample, the results clearly demonstrate that high quality mitochondria preparations can
be obtained from pressurized, rather than homogenized, samples Although mitochondria are more difficult to isolate from fibrous tissue, such as muscle, than
from softer tissues such as liver, mitochondrial function and dysfunction in muscle tissue
have been studied extensively in recent years The methods for isolation of muscle
mitochondria have changed very little over the past three decades [4,9,10], even though a
better and more consistent method for isolation of intact muscle mitochondria would greatly
benefit this area of research An instrument-based tissue disruption method, capable of
processing multiple samples under essentially identical conditions, is expected to lead to
more consistent results, and substantial savings of time and resources Muscle tissue
disruption in The PCT Shredder (or The Shredder SG3, both from Pressure BioSciences,
Inc) requires approximately ten seconds per sample and can be performed by an operator
with minimal training and experience Here we present methods for extraction and assay of rat skeletal muscle mitochondria that
do not rely upon a manual, user-regulated homogenization step In addition, several
parameters of the new method were also tested with kidney mitochondria to demonstrate
that the methods are likely to be broadly applicable to mitochondria from other tissues The
results confirm that mitochondria extracted using pressure cycling, or The PCT Shredder, or
a combination of both methods, are intact and functionally active, and can be used for
downstream applications that require high quality, enriched mitochondria preparations.Gross et al Page 2
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author ManuscriptMaterials and Methods
Animals
Male, specific pathogen free, Fischer 344 × Brown Norway F1 (FBNF1) rats were obtained
from Harlan (South Easton, MA) at 7–9 weeks of age Tissues were taken from animals on
other study protocols so as to avoid unnecessary animal use Animals were housed in pairs
and maintained in a room with constant humidity and a 12 hour light-dark cycle, and fed ad
libitum  Animals were sacrificed by rapid decapitation after 8 weeks (at 4 months of age) Following decapitation, both kidneys, or one hind limb gastrocnemius muscle, were quickly
removed and placed in ice-cold isolation buffer All subsequent steps of homogenization and
centrifugation were carried out at 4°C Since we can only work up at most two samples/day (given animal availability), all studies
were done on different days in samples from different animals, unless noted (see Table 1) Chemicals
All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co (St Louis, MO), except
where indicated otherwise Isolation of mitochondria from kidney tissue
The procedure was based on the protocol for isolation of liver mitochondria used in our
group [11] To cool the tissue rapidly and preserve mitochondrial function, adult rat kidneys
(~2 g total) were rapidly dissected and placed into semi-frozen slush of N1 buffer, composed
of 250 mM sucrose, 1 mM EGTA, 10 mM HEPES, (final pH adjusted to 7.4 with KOH),
supplemented with 0.5% BSA (essentially fatty acid-free) In some experiments (specifically
those denoted as “ original protocol ” in Table 3), the kidneys were thoroughly minced with
scissors in N1 buffer to wash away blood, the capsule and medulla were removed and the
minced tissue was split evenly into three FT-500 PULSE Tubes (Pressure BioSciences, Inc.,
South Easton, MA) In the simplified protocol , each kidney was cut into ~6 pieces in N1
buffer and the capsule and medulla were removed The intact tissue pieces were placed
directly into 3 FT-500 PULSE Tubes (~0.6–0.7 g tissue per tube) For all samples, the
PULSE Tube rams were gently inserted and pushed up until the tissue was just compacted
between the ram and the lysis disk, forcing extra blood and buffer out through the lysis disk
holes This liquid was withdrawn and discarded to reduce the amount of blood carry-over
into the sample The ram was then pushed up further, forcing the tissue through the holes of
the lysis disk Fresh N1 buffer, 1 ml per PULSE Tube, was added to each sample bringing
the volume up to 1.4 ml In some experiments reported only in the supplemental material,
N1 was supplemented with 5% dextran in the pressure cycling step, because preliminary
experiments had suggested that the quality of mitochondria extracted from rat liver by
pressure cycling could be improved by the addition of 5% dextran

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "To cool the tissue rapidly and preserve mitochondrial function, adult rat kidneys (~2 g total) were rapidly dissected and placed into semi-frozen slush of N1 buffer, composed of 250 mM sucrose, 1 mM EGTA, 10 mM HEPES, (final pH adjusted to 7.4 with KOH), supplemented with 0.5% BSA (essentially fatty acid-free)."
2. "In the simplified protocol, each kidney was cut into ~6 pieces in N1 buffer and the capsule and medulla were removed."
3. "The intact tissue pieces were placed directly into 3 FT-500 PULSE Tubes (~0.6–0.7 g tissue per tube)."
4. "For all samples, the PULSE Tube rams were gently inserted and pushed up until the tissue was just compacted between the ram and the lysis disk, forcing extra blood and buffer out through the lysis disk holes."
5. "This liquid was withdrawn and discarded to reduce the amount of blood carry-over into the sample."
6. "The ram was then pushed up further, forcing the tissue through the holes of the lysis disk."
7. "Fresh N1 buffer, 1 ml per PULSE Tube, was added to each sample bringing the volume up to 1.4 ml."
8. "In some experiments reported only in the supplemental material, N1 was supplemented with 5% dextran in the pressure cycling step, because preliminary experiments had suggested that the quality of mitochondria extracted from rat liver by pressure cycling could be improved by the addition of 5% dextran."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not mention the specific steps for homogenization and centrifugation.
- The article does not provide details on the pressure cycling step or the duration of the process.
- The article does not mention the specific steps for the subsequent isolation of mitochondria from the PULSE Tubes.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some steps for the isolation of mitochondria from rat kidney tissue using the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling. However, it lacks information on important steps such as homogenization, centrifugation, and the specific protocol for isolating mitochondria from the PULSE Tubes. Additionally, details about the pressure cycling step and the duration of the process are missing. Therefore, the protocol is incomplete, and further information is needed to fully understand the mitochondrial isolation procedure.

summary: Authors have used nitrogen cavitation to isolate mitochondria from cultured cells. This method involves pressurizing the sample and then rapidly decompressing it to form gas bubbles. Although it requires cleaning the pressure chamber between samples, it yields high-quality mitochondria. Isolating mitochondria from muscle tissue is more challenging, but a new instrument-based method called The PCT Shredder shows promise. It allows for consistent processing of multiple samples and requires minimal training. The method has been successfully tested with rat skeletal muscle and kidney mitochondria, producing intact and functional mitochondria for downstream applications.

However, initial results
indicated that kidney mitochondria pressure cycled at in the presence of 5% dextran showed
only a borderline improvement in RCR and ADP/O ratio compared to those extracted at the
same pressure without dextran (see supplemental material) Therefore, dextran was omitted
from all subsequent kidney mitochondria preparations In Table 2, equivalent data generated
with and without dextran were pooled Pressure cycling was performed at 4°C for 5, 10 or 20 cycles in an NEP 2320 Barocycler
(Pressure BioSciences, Inc.) Each cycle consisted of 20 seconds at high pressure (10,000 or
20,000 psi) and 5 seconds at atmospheric pressure After pressure cycling, the three tissue homogenates were pooled and transferred to round
bottom, 32.5 ml centrifuge tubes (e.g., Beckman 355642) PULSE Tubes were rinsed with 1Gross et al Page 3
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptml each of fresh N1, the rinses were combined with the homogenate adjusted to
approximately 20 mL and centrifuged in a fixed angle rotor at 1000 × g for 8 minutes to
pellet intact cells, nuclei and large debris The supernatant was transferred to a clean round
bottom tube and centrifuged at 14,000 × g for 8 minutes at 4°C to pellet the mitochondria-
enriched fraction This pellet was resuspended in a 0.5 ml aliquot of fresh N1 and gently
homogenized using 5–10 strokes in a 1 or 2 ml glass homogenizer with a Teflon pestle The
round bottom centrifuge tube and homogenizer were rinsed with a second 1ml aliquot of
fresh N1 and the two aliquots were combined The pooled mitochondrial suspension (~1.5
ml total) was transferred to a 1.5 ml microfuge tube Mitochondria were centrifuged at
14,000 × g and the supernatant was discarded In order to wash away the BSA from the N1
buffer, the pellet was resuspended in 0.5 ml N2 (250 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.4)
using the plastic pestle provided with the microfuge tube In some experiments, (specifically
those denoted as “original protocol” in Table 3) the suspension was transferred to a 1 or 2 ml
glass homogenizer, gently homogenized using 5–10 strokes and then transferred to a new
tube In the simplified protocol, this homogenization step was omitted The first tube and the
homogenizer were rinsed with a second 1 ml aliquot of fresh N2 and the rinse was added to
the mitochondrial suspension The sample was then centrifuged again at 14,000 × g and the
supernatant was discarded To this final pellet, 30 μl of N2 was added and the pellet was
gently and thoroughly homogenized with the plastic pestle The final volume of the kidney
mitochondria-enriched fraction was ~100–250 μl For preparation of kidney mitochondria by mechanical disruption with The PCT Shredder
(Pressure BioSciences, Inc.), the metal shredder base was pre-chilled by placing it into a
−20°C freezer 10–30 minutes prior to the start of tissue collection Each kidney was cut into
~6 pieces in N1 buffer and the capsule and medulla were removed The intact tissue pieces
were placed directly into 3 FT 500-S Shredder PULSE Tubes (Figure 1) (0.6–0.7 g tissue
per tube) and the rams were gently inserted and pushed up until the tissue was just
compacted between the ram and the lysis disk Extra blood and buffer was withdrawn as
described above N1 buffer (0.5ml per tube) was added and the samples were shredded for
10 seconds with the pre-cooled PCT Shredder After shredding, the homogenate was
transferred to a Beckman round bottom centrifuge tube The Shredder tubes were rinsed
with additional N1, the rinses were combined with the homogenate and centrifuged at 1000
× g for 8 minutes at 4°C All subsequent steps were performed as described above for the

homogenate was transferred to a round bottom centrifuge tube The homogenizer was rinsed
with 3 ml of fresh N1, the rinse was combined with the homogenate and centrifuged at 1000
× g for 8 minutes at 4°C All subsequent steps were performed as described above Isolation of mitochondria from skeletal muscle
Methodology for extraction of mitochondria from skeletal muscle was based loosely on the
simplified protocol that was established for kidney mitochondria The gastrocnemius muscle
was rapidly excised and placed into semi-frozen slush of Muscle Mitochondria Isolation
Buffer (MMIB; 120 mM KCl, 5 mM MgCl 2, 1 mM EGTA, 20 mM HEPES, pH adjusted to
7.4 with KOH, as described in [12] ) in order to rapidly cool the tissue All subsequent steps
were performed at 4°C

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for the isolation of kidney mitochondria:

1. "Pressure cycling was performed at 4°C for 5, 10 or 20 cycles in an NEP 2320 Barocycler (Pressure BioSciences, Inc.) Each cycle consisted of 20 seconds at high pressure (10,000 or 20,000 psi) and 5 seconds at atmospheric pressure."
2. "After pressure cycling, the three tissue homogenates were pooled and transferred to round bottom, 32.5 ml centrifuge tubes (e.g., Beckman 355642)."
3. "The tubes were rinsed with 1 ml each of fresh N1, and the rinses were combined with the homogenate adjusted to approximately 20 mL."
4. "The homogenate was centrifuged in a fixed angle rotor at 1000 × g for 8 minutes to pellet intact cells, nuclei, and large debris."
5. "The supernatant was transferred to a clean round bottom tube and centrifuged at 14,000 × g for 8 minutes at 4°C to pellet the mitochondria-enriched fraction."
6. "This pellet was resuspended in a 0.5 ml aliquot of fresh N1 and gently homogenized using 5–10 strokes in a 1 or 2 ml glass homogenizer with a Teflon pestle."
7. "The round bottom centrifuge tube and homogenizer were rinsed with a second 1 ml aliquot of fresh N1, and the two aliquots were combined."
8. "The pooled mitochondrial suspension (~1.5 ml total) was transferred to a 1.5 ml microfuge tube."
9. "Mitochondria were centrifuged at 14,000 × g and the supernatant was discarded."
10. "The pellet was resuspended in 0.5 ml N2 (250 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.4) using the plastic pestle provided with the microfuge tube."
11. "In some experiments, the suspension was transferred to a 1 or 2 ml glass homogenizer, gently homogenized using 5–10 strokes, and then transferred to a new tube."
12. "In the simplified protocol, this homogenization step was omitted."
13. "The first tube and the homogenizer were rinsed with a second 1 ml aliquot of fresh N2, and the rinse was added to the mitochondrial suspension."
14. "The sample was then centrifuged again at 14,000 × g and the supernatant was discarded."
15. "To the final pellet, 30 μl of N2 was added, and the pellet was gently and thoroughly homogenized with the plastic pestle."
16. "The final volume of the kidney mitochondria-enriched fraction was ~100–250 μl."

Steps for the isolation of kidney mitochondria using The PCT Shredder:

1. "The metal shredder base was pre-chilled by placing it into a −20°C freezer 10–30 minutes prior to the start of tissue collection."
2. "Each kidney was cut into ~6 pieces in N1 buffer, and the capsule and medulla were removed."
3. "The intact tissue pieces were placed directly into 3 FT 500-S Shredder PULSE Tubes (0.6–0.7 g tissue per tube), and the rams were gently inserted and pushed up until the tissue was just compacted between the ram and the lysis disk."
4. "N1 buffer (0.5 ml per tube) was added, and the samples were shredded for 10 seconds with the pre-cooled PCT Shredder."
5. "After shredding, the homogenate was transferred to a Beckman round bottom centrifuge tube."

summary: Revised Summary: Initial results of RCR measurement in kidney mitochondria pressure cycled with 5% dextran showed only a borderline improvement compared to those without dextran. Dextran was omitted from subsequent kidney mitochondria preparations. Pressure cycling was performed at 4°C for 5, 10, or 20 cycles using an NEP 2320 Barocycler. The tissue homogenates were pooled, and the mitochondria-enriched fraction was obtained through centrifugation in Beckman 355642 centrifuge tubes. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 14,000 × g for 8 minutes at 4°C. The mitochondrial suspension was transferred to a 1.5 ml microfuge tube and centrifuged again. The final pellet was resuspended in N2 buffer using a plastic pestle. The volume of the kidney mitochondria-enriched fraction was approximately 100-250 μl. Kidney mitochondria were also prepared using The PCT Shredder by Pressure BioSciences. The metal shredder base was pre-chilled in a -20°C freezer. The kidney tissue was cut into pieces and the medulla was removed. The tissue was then shredded using FT 500-S Shredder PULSE Tubes. Control kidney mitochondria were prepared by homogenizing minced kidney tissue in N1 buffer. Mitochondria were also isolated from skeletal muscle using a similar protocol. All steps were performed at 4°C.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Weigh the tissue (1.5-1.8 g each).
2. Roughly mince the tissue with scissors in MMIB and remove large pieces of tendon.
3. Further mince the tissue while incubating for 5 minutes in 0.25 mg/ml Nagarse diluted in MMIB.
4. Pour off the Nagarse solution and wash the tissue twice in ice-cold MMIB.
5. Split the minced tissue into 8 FT 500-S Shredder PULSE Tubes.
6. Shred each tissue aliquot for 10 seconds with a prechilled PCT Shredder in 0.5 ml MMIB.
7. Bring the volume in each tube up to 1.4 ml with additional MMIB.
8. Subject the samples to pressure cycling at 4°C.
9. Perform pressure cycling at 10,000, 20,000, or 30,000 psi for 5 or 10 cycles.
10. Each cycle consists of 20 seconds at high pressure and 5 seconds at atmospheric pressure.
11. Pool the tissue homogenates and transfer them to round bottom centrifuge tubes.
12. Rinse each PULSE Tube with 1 ml of fresh MMIB and combine the rinses with the homogenate.
13. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1000 × g for 8 minutes at 4°C to pellet intact cells, nuclei, and large debris.
14. Transfer the supernatant (except for the small amount of milky suspension floating just above the pellet) to a fresh round bottom tube.
15. Centrifuge the supernatant at 14,000 × g for 8 minutes at 4°C to pellet the mitochondria-enriched fraction.
16. Note: In some experiments, the speed was reduced to 7,500 or 10,000 × g.
17. After centrifugation, the mitochondrial pellet consists of two layers: a darker bottom layer containing intact mitochondria and a pale top layer.
18. Gently tap the bottom of the tube to separate the layers, causing the top layer to slide down the side of the tube while the lower layer remains attached.
19. Gently aspirate and discard the pale material (saved in control experiments for further analysis).
20. Resuspend the remaining dark pellet in 0.5 ml of fresh MMIB and gently homogenize using 5-10 strokes in a 1 or 2 ml glass homogenizer with a Teflon pestle.
21. Rinse the round bottom centrifuge tube and homogenizer with a second 1 ml aliquot of fresh MMIB and combine the two aliquots.
22. Transfer the pooled mitochondrial suspension (~1.5 ml total) to a 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube with a matching pestle.
23. Centrifuge the mitochondria at 14,000 × g and discard the supernatant.
24. Resuspend the pellet with 30 μl fresh MMIB and homogenize with the plastic pestle to form a uniform suspension.
25. The final volume of the mitochondria-enriched fraction is ~70-120 μL.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide details on the composition of MMIB.
- The article does not specify the duration of incubation with Nagarse.
- The article does not mention the specific type of glass homogenizer used.
- The article does not provide information on the respiration buffer composition for the mitochondrial respiration assays.

**Conclusion:**
The protocol for mitochondrial isolation is described in detail, including tissue preparation, enzymatic digestion, shredding, pressure cycling, centrifugation, and resuspension steps. However, there are some protocol gaps, such as missing details on the composition of MMIB, the duration of incubation with

summary:
Iteration 4:
Summary: The tissue was weighed and minced with scissors in MMIB, removing large pieces of tendon. It was then incubated with Nagarse proteinase XXIV for 5 minutes and washed in ice-cold MMIB. The minced tissue was split into 8 FT 500-S Shredder PULSE Tubes and shredded for 10 seconds in 0.5 ml MMIB. After shredding, the volume was adjusted to 1.4 ml with MMIB and subjected to pressure cycling using the PCT Shredder. The tissue homogenates were combined, centrifuged, and the supernatant was transferred to a fresh tube. The mitochondria-enriched fraction, containing intact mitochondria, was pelleted and resuspended in fresh MMIB. The resuspended pellet was homogenized and combined with a second aliquot of fresh MMIB. The pooled mitochondrial suspension (~1.5 ml total) was transferred to a 1.5 ml microcentrifuge tube. Mitochondria were centrifuged and the supernatant was discarded. The pellet was resuspended with fresh MMIB to form a uniform suspension. The final volume of the mitochondria-enriched fraction was approximately 70-120 μL. For control mitochondria, muscle tissue was harvested, minced, and incubated with Nagarse proteinase XXIV. The homogenate was poured off and fresh MMIB was added for further homogenization. The homogenates were combined and centrifuged to pellet intact cells, nuclei, and large debris. Electron microscopy was performed on fixed mitochondria. Mitochondrial respiration was measured using the Oroboros Oxygraph-2k system, which allows for simultaneous measurement of two samples in adjacent chambers. The respiration buffer used contained 130 mM sucrose, 5 mM HEPES, 5 mM KH 2PO4, 50 μM EGTA, and 5 mM MgCl 2.

addition of mitochondria Optimization of respiration buffer for muscle mitochondria is described in the Results
section Isolated skeletal muscle mitochondria were incubated in optimized respiration
buffer composed of 10 mM KH 2PO4, 25 μM EGTA, 125 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl, 25 mM
HEPES, adjusted to pH 7.2 with KOH (based on [14], with multiple modifications, see
below) Respiration assays consisted of adding either respiratory complex I substrate (5 mM
glutamate/5 mM malate) or respiratory complex II substrate (5 mM succinate/2 μM
Rotenone) prior to addition of mitochondria The respiration buffer was supplemented with
1 mM MgCl 2 for use with respiratory complex II substrate (see results) Evaluation of the rates of respiration at state 2 (V2 per Estabrook [15], not per Chance and
Williams, [16]), state 3 (V3), state 4 (V4) and fully uncoupled respiration (Vu) was done
through sequential additions of respiratory substrate, 100 μM ADP and 0.1 μM of FCCP,
respectively ADP/O and respiratory control ratios (RCR) were determined either as the ratio
V3/V4 (RCR 3/4) or as the ratio V3/V2 (RCR 3/2) Multiparameter kinetic assessment of mitochondrial physiological parameters isolated
from skeletal muscle
Simultaneous measurements of mitochondrial swelling, Ca2+ fluxes, change in membrane
potential ( Δϕm), and changes of pyridine nucleotide oxidation (NAD(P)H) were performed
at a mitochondria concentration of 100 μg protein/ml using a multichannel fluorescent setup
as previously described; [17] Mitochondria were energized with 5 mM succinate and
challenged with a series of bolus additions of Ca2+ (15 μM each) Assays were carried out in
buffer containing 250 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, 1 mM KH 2PO4, and 2.5 μM EDTA
adjusted to pH 7.4 with KOH A decrease in autofluorescence at λex/λem = 360/440 nm was
used to detect changes in NAD(P)H oxidation Ca2+ fluxes were detected using Ca-Green
5N (Invitrogen, Carlsbad Ca.), a non-permeable fluorescent dye, while
tetramethylrhodamine methyl ester (TMRM) (Invitrogen) was used in self-quenching mode
to detect Δϕm Mitochondrial swelling was detected as a decrease in light scattering at
excitation and emission wavelengths of 587 nm measured at an angle of 90° Calcium
retention capacity (CRC) was determined as the amount of exogenously added Ca2+
required to induc e mitochondrial swelling and decline of other functions, e.g., mitochondrial
depolarization, Ca2+ release and oxidation of pyridine nucleotides Under these conditions,
mitochondrial swelling was essentially complete, based on subsequent alamethicin exposure
which increased swelling by <15% (data not shown) The mPT involvement in Ca2+-
induced mitochondrial swelling was assessed in the presence of cyclosporin A Two dimensional gel electrophoresis
Recovery of total mitochondrial protein was measured by BCA assay.Gross et al Page 6

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Optimization of respiration buffer for muscle mitochondria:
   - Composed of 10 mM KH 2PO4, 25 μM EGTA, 125 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl, 25 mM HEPES
   - Adjusted to pH 7.2 with KOH
   - Based on [14], with multiple modifications

2. Respiration assays:
   - Add either respiratory complex I substrate (5 mM glutamate/5 mM malate) or respiratory complex II substrate (5 mM succinate/2 μM Rotenone)
   - Add mitochondria

3. Supplement respiration buffer with 1 mM MgCl2 for use with respiratory complex II substrate.

4. Evaluation of rates of respiration:
   - State 2 (V2 per Estabrook [15])
   - State 3 (V3)
   - State 4 (V4)
   - Fully uncoupled respiration (Vu)
   - Sequential additions of respiratory substrate, 100 μM ADP, and 0.1 μM FCCP, respectively

5. Calculation of ADP/O and respiratory control ratios (RCR):
   - ADP/O: ratio V3/V4
   - RCR: ratio V3/V2

6. Simultaneous measurements of mitochondrial swelling, Ca2+ fluxes, change in membrane potential (Δϕm), and changes of pyridine nucleotide oxidation (NAD(P)H):
   - Mitochondria concentration: 100 μg protein/ml
   - Energize mitochondria with 5 mM succinate
   - Challenge with a series of bolus additions of Ca2+ (15 μM each)
   - Assays carried out in buffer containing 250 mM sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, 1 mM KH 2PO4, and 2.5 μM EDTA, adjusted to pH 7.4 with KOH
   - Detection of NAD(P)H oxidation: decrease in autofluorescence at λex/λem = 360/440 nm
   - Detection of Ca2+ fluxes: Ca-Green 5N fluorescent dye
   - Detection of Δϕm: tetramethylrhodamine methyl ester (TMRM) in self-quenching mode
   - Detection of mitochondrial swelling: decrease in light scattering at excitation and emission wavelengths of 587 nm measured at an angle of 90°
   - Determination of calcium retention capacity (CRC): amount of exogenously added Ca2+ required to induce mitochondrial swelling and decline of other functions

7. Assessment of mPT involvement in Ca2+-induced mitochondrial swelling in the presence of cyclosporin A.

8. Two-dimensional gel electrophoresis:
   - Desalting prior to isoelectric focusing (IEF) for muscle mitochondria 2D gels
   - Desalting performed by diluting samples in ProteoSolve-IEF supplemented with 50 mM DTT and loading onto Amicon Ultra centrifugal filters with 3 kDa molecular weight cutoff
   - Protein concentration measured by Bradford assay

9. Loading of 200 μg of mitochondrial suspension per gel for all 2D gels.

10. First dimension separation on the computer-controlled IsoelectrIQ2 IEF apparatus using 11cm, pH 3–10 ReadyStrip IPG strips.
   - Separation programmed with current limited to 50 μA per strip in two steps: twelve hours on a concave voltage ramp set to start at 100V and end at 1000V, and 8 hours at 1000V.
   - Strips removed when 90kVh was reached.

article:
Chunk # 5.
For Western blotting, proteins were transferred
to Immobilon-P membrane (EMD-Millipore) and blocked with 5% non-fat milk in tris-
buffered saline with 0.05% Tween-20 Primary antibodies were diluted in blocking solution Blots were incubated with 1° antibody either for 4 hours at room temperature or overnight at
4°C Detection was carried out using HRP-coupled secondary antibodies and enhanced
chemiluminescence (ECL Advance kit from GE, Piscataway, NJ) Results
Effect of pressure cycling on muscle and kidney mitochondria
Prior to optimizing PCT Shredder and Barocycler-based protocols for isolation of
mitochondria, we tested whether exposure of muscle mitochondria to high hydrostatic
pressure cycling was detrimental to mitochondrial function Muscle mitochondria were
prepared using the standard Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer method The mitochondrial pellet
was resuspended in 1.5 ml of the isolation buffer and split into 2 aliquots One aliquot was
transferred to an FT 500-ND diskless pulse tube and subjected to pressure cycling at 10,000
psi for 5 cycles The control aliquot was kept on ice After pressure treatment, both aliquots
were centrifuged and the mitochondrial pellets were resuspended in 30 μl of MMIB buffer The results (Table 1) from matched controls (n=2), indicate that exposure to pressure
cycling at 10,000 psi is compatible with normal respiration kinetics as measured byGross et al Page 7
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptrespiratory control ratio (RCR) and ADP/O ratio of isolated mitochondria While the N is
small, we note that this result is consistently observed, as will be seen below The effect of pressure cycling on kidney mitochondria respiration was also assayed Mitochondria were extracted from fresh rat kidney tissue using either the standard manual
homogenizer method, or PCT Shredder alone, or by pressure cycling alone The respiratory
control ratios (measured using complex I substrate) clearly show that pressure cycling at
10,000 psi has no measurable effect on kidney mitochondria respiration, compared to the
control samples or the samples extracted using the PCT Shredder alone (Table 2) Kidney
mitochondria extracted using 20,000 psi demonstrate only a slight reduction in both RCR
3/2 and RCR 3/4, suggesting that this pressure may also be compatible with isolation of
intact mitochondria Simplification of tissue pre-processing for isolation of mitochondria
The initial step in mitochondrial isolation, rinsing to remove excess blood and mincing to
facilitate homogenization, is inherently problematic since it occurs at a time when the
mitochondria are at their most vulnerable due to temperature and surrounding milieu, e.g.,
the presence of tissue-derived proteases and divalent cations Initially, we considered
whether this step, common to essentially all mitochondrial isolation protocols, would also be
required for clean/high quality mitochondria preparations by our methods Since tissue
processing in the PULSE Tube includes a mechanical disruption step when the tissue is
forced through the holes of the lysis disk (either with or without the PCT Shredder,
depending on the tissue type), we tested whether this quick and easy step could be
substituted for the manual mincing We used kidney tissue for these tests because it is much
less fibrous than muscle, and can be forced through the holes in the PULSE Tube lysis disk,
effectively mincing the tissue with one quick push of the ram This enabled us to investigate
this issue, PULSE Tube-facilitated mincing, without complications associated with the
protease treatment typically used for skeletal muscle (mechanical homogenization of muscle
tissue releases primarily subsarcolemmal mitochondria -- treatment with the protease
Nagarse is required for efficient extraction of the intermyofibrillar mitochondria fraction
[10,19] Therefore, to extract both subpopulations of muscle mitochondria, muscle samples
were always minced in the enzyme prior to further homogenization) We also note that
kidney, in contrast to skeletal muscle, has a significant amount of blood in the organ,
enabling us to use kidney to examine whether this PULSE Tube-facilitated mincing is
compatible with effective removal of blood from the sample In addition to extensive tissue mincing, our original kidney protocol for mitochondria
extraction included two wash steps at which the mitochondria-enriched fraction was
resuspended and washed in a glass homogenizer with a Teflon pestle Since additional steps
increase processing time and sample loss due to multiple transfers, the second of the two
homogenizer washes was omitted in the simplified kidney protocol The respiratory control ratios of the kidney mitochondria prepared by the original protocol
were compared to those obtained by the simplified protocol (Table 3) The results indicate
that the simplified protocol yields mitochondria that are very similar to the ones obtained
with the more involved original protocol and support the use of the PULSE Tube lysis disk,
in place of manual mincing, for tissue disruption of relatively soft tissues such as kidney

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Muscle mitochondria were prepared using the standard Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer method.
2. The mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in 1.5 ml of the isolation buffer.
3. The resuspended mitochondria were split into 2 aliquots.
4. One aliquot was transferred to an FT 500-ND diskless pulse tube and subjected to pressure cycling at 10,000 psi for 5 cycles.
5. The control aliquot was kept on ice.
6. After pressure treatment, both aliquots were centrifuged.
7. The mitochondrial pellets were resuspended in 30 μl of MMIB buffer.
8. Kidney mitochondria were extracted from fresh rat kidney tissue using either the standard manual homogenizer method, PCT Shredder alone, or pressure cycling alone.
9. The respiratory control ratios of the kidney mitochondria were measured using complex I substrate.
10. The results showed that pressure cycling at 10,000 psi had no measurable effect on kidney mitochondria respiration compared to the control samples or the samples extracted using the PCT Shredder alone.
11. Kidney mitochondria extracted using 20,000 psi demonstrated only a slight reduction in both RCR 3/2 and RCR 3/4, suggesting that this pressure may also be compatible with isolation of intact mitochondria.
12. The initial step in mitochondrial isolation, rinsing to remove excess blood and mincing to facilitate homogenization, was tested for substitution with PULSE Tube-facilitated mincing.
13. Kidney tissue was forced through the holes of the PULSE Tube lysis disk to effectively mince the tissue.
14. The PULSE Tube-facilitated mincing was tested without complications associated with protease treatment typically used for skeletal muscle.
15. The simplified protocol for kidney mitochondria extraction omitted the second of the two homogenizer washes.
16. The respiratory control ratios of the kidney mitochondria prepared by the original protocol were compared to those obtained by the simplified protocol.
17. The results indicated that the simplified protocol yielded mitochondria that were very similar to the ones obtained with the more involved original protocol.
18. The use of the PULSE Tube lysis disk, in place of manual mincing, was supported for tissue disruption of relatively soft tissues such as kidney.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides detailed steps for the isolation of muscle and kidney mitochondria using different methods. The muscle mitochondria were isolated using the standard Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer method, followed by pressure cycling at 10,000 psi. The kidney mitochondria were extracted using the standard manual homogenizer method, PCT Shredder alone, or pressure cycling alone. The article also suggests the use of PULSE Tube-facilitated mincing as a substitute for manual mincing in the isolation of kidney mitochondria. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, as some steps are missing or not clearly described.

Chunk # 6.
In addition, since the respiration of stable mitochondria preparations should change little
over the course of several hours, while unstable damaged mitochondria should exhibit a
significant drop in function, the kidney mitochondria prepared by pressure cycling at 10,000
psi using either the original or the simplified protocol, were assayed for stability Initial
respiration readings were performed within ~2 hours of mitochondria isolation, and secondGross et al Page 8
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptreadings were performed after the preps had been stored on ice for an additional 3–4 hours The results (see supplemental material) indicate that kidney mitochondria isolated by
pressure cycling at 10,000 psi using either the original or the simplified protocols are equally
stable over the time course studied Respiration buffer formulation for skeletal muscle mitochondria respiration assay
Having confirmed that pressure cycling does not disrupt the function or stability of kidney
mitochondria, we then proceeded to extend this methodology and apply it to extraction of
functional mitochondria from skeletal muscle Since many different formulations of
respiration buffer for rat muscle mitochondria have been reported in the literature [20–22]
we first wanted to determine what respiration buffer formulation gave us the best RCR
values for skeletal muscle mitochondria, before proceeding to optimization of the extraction
methodology This was done so as to best highlight mitochondrial functional status While it
was impractical to exhaustively test and compare all the different buffer formulations, we
began testing certain components of the respiration buffer using the formulation described
by Skalska at al [22] as the starting point (1 mM KH 2PO4, 50 μM EDTA, 1 mM MgCl 2,
125 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl, 25 mM HEPES, pH 7.2) A pilot study indicated that the
presence of MgCl 2, while necessary for respiration with complex II substrate (succinate),
was detrimental to respiration with complex I substrate (glutamate+malate) This pilot
involved splitting a single sample of purified muscle mitochondria into two aliquots and
assaying one half in buffer containing 1 mM MgCl 2, and the other half in the same buffer
without MgCl 2 Higher RCRs, especially in RCR 3/4 (>6 vs <4), were obtained when
MgCl 2 was omitted from the respiration buffer At the same time, the efficiency of both
mitochondrial samples as judged by ADP/O ratio was comparable, indicating that in both
cases mitochondria were similarly intact This was not investigated further, and we
continued to omit MgCl 2 from complex I respiration experiments, as have others [21,23,24] In addition, muscle mitochondria were assayed with glutamate+malate in respiration buffer
containing 1 mM or 10 mM KH 2PO4 Increasing the KH 2PO4 concentration from 1 mM to
10 mM significantly increased both RCR 3/2 and RCR 3/4, as shown in Table 4 Therefore,
for subsequent muscle mitochondria assays, 10 mM KH 2PO4 was included in the respiration
buffer The effect of chelating agent choice and concentration on muscle mitochondria respiration
was also tested While the effect of chelator did not have as pronounced an effect as
KH2PO4 and MgCl 2 concentration, it was determined that switching from 50 μM EDTA to
25 μM EGTA was slightly beneficial (data not shown) The final muscle mitochondria
respiration buffer formulation was briefly compared to the initial recipe [14] and was shown
to result in improvement of RCR with respiratory complex I substrate (Table 5) Again, this
was not systematically or extensively examined, as this was not the focus of the current
study Effect of centrifugation speed on yield and quality of skeletal muscle mitochondria
To further examine the experimental parameters that yield the highest quality mitochondria
preparations, we next investigated whether altering the centrifugation speed used to
sediment the mitochondria could be used to increase either yield or purity Accordingly,
skeletal muscle mitochondria were initially prepared using three spin speeds for the first
high speed spin The data in Table 6 suggest that the lower yield in PCT-isolated
mitochondria is not generally associated with more debris/fragmented mitochondria (which
would have been predicted to be disproportionally isolated with the 14,000 × g spin) Since
there was no clear gain in protein recovery at higher g-force (Table 6), respiration was next
compared using samples prepared by PCT at 10,000 psi and centrifuged at 7,500 × g or
14,000 × g to determine whether the slower spin resulted in better mitochondria preparationsGross et al Page 9
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptas a result of fewer contaminants (Table 7) The RCR values (RCR 3/2 and RCR 3/4)
indicated that muscle mitochondria pelleted at 14,000 × g were as good as, or slightly better
than, those prepared using 7,500 × g

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The kidney mitochondria were prepared by pressure cycling at 10,000 psi using either the original or simplified protocol.
2. Initial respiration readings were performed within ~2 hours of mitochondria isolation.
3. Second readings were performed after the preps had been stored on ice for an additional 3-4 hours.
4. The results indicate that kidney mitochondria isolated by pressure cycling at 10,000 psi using either the original or simplified protocols are equally stable over the time course studied.
5. The respiration buffer formulation for skeletal muscle mitochondria respiration assay was optimized.
6. Different formulations of respiration buffer for rat muscle mitochondria were tested.
7. The starting point formulation was 1 mM KH2PO4, 50 μM EDTA, 1 mM MgCl2, 125 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl, 25 mM HEPES, pH 7.2.
8. It was found that the presence of MgCl2 was detrimental to respiration with complex I substrate (glutamate+malate).
9. Omitting MgCl2 from the respiration buffer resulted in higher RCRs for complex I respiration experiments.
10. Muscle mitochondria were assayed with glutamate+malate in respiration buffer containing 1 mM or 10 mM KH2PO4.
11. Increasing the KH2PO4 concentration from 1 mM to 10 mM significantly increased both RCR 3/2 and RCR 3/4.

16. The effect of centrifugation speed on yield and quality of skeletal muscle mitochondria was investigated.

summary:
The stability of kidney mitochondria prepared by pressure cycling at 10,000 psi using either the original or simplified protocol was assessed. Initial respiration readings were taken within ~2 hours of isolation, and second readings were performed after storing the samples on ice for an additional 3-4 hours. The results show that both protocols yield equally stable kidney mitochondria over the studied time course. 

To optimize the extraction of functional mitochondria from skeletal muscle, different formulations of respiration buffer were tested. Starting with a formulation described in the literature, certain components of the buffer were modified. It was found that the presence of MgCl2 was detrimental to respiration with complex I substrate, while increasing KH2PO4 concentration significantly improved respiration. The choice and concentration of chelating agent also had a slight effect on muscle mitochondria respiration. 

The effect of centrifugation speed on the yield and quality of skeletal muscle mitochondria was investigated. Three different spin speeds were tested, and it was observed that the lower yield in pressure cycling technology (PCT)-isolated mitochondria was not associated with more debris or fragmented mitochondria. Comparing different spin speeds, it was found that muscle mitochondria pelleted at 14,000 × g were as good as, or slightly better than, those prepared using 7,500 × g.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation using the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling:

1. Disrupt muscle tissue for 10 seconds using the PCT Shredder.
2. Use the crude homogenate for mitochondria isolation by differential centrifugation (Shredder alone), or subject it to further extraction by pressure cycling for five cycles at 10,000, 20,000, or 30,000 psi to release additional mitochondria from cells.
3. Assay mitochondrial yield and respiration and compare to control samples extracted using a standard glass homogenizer with a Teflon pestle.
4. Protein assay of the mitochondria-enriched pellet shows that disruption with the PCT Shredder followed by pressure cycling releases more mitochondria compared to the Shredder alone, approaching the yield obtained using the manual homogenizer method.
5. Little additional yield is recovered at higher pressures.
6. Respiration assays confirm that mitochondria exposed to a hydrostatic pressure of 10,000 psi exhibit normal respiration kinetics with both complex I and complex II substrates.
7. Pressure cycling at 30,000 psi results in damaged mitochondria, indicated by low RCR values.
8. Mitochondria exposed to 20,000 psi appear almost as healthy as controls, indicating a wide range of pressures compatible with viable mitochondria.
9. Compare mitochondria isolated using the conventional homogenizer method and the PCT Shredder with pressure cycling at 10,000 psi to assess the ability of muscle mitochondria to respond to Ca2+ overload.
10. Skeletal muscle mitochondria prepared by both methods appear comparable in their membrane potential, ability to retain exogenously added calcium, redox level of pyridine nucleotides, and Ca2+-induced swelling rate/amplitude.
11. The involvement of mitochondrial permeability transition (mPT) in Ca2+-induced mitochondrial dysfunction was assessed in the presence of cyclosporine A (CsA), but this was not investigated in detail.
12. Assess the morphology and purity of mitochondria extracted using the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling.
13. Transmission electron microscopy confirms that the mitochondria extracted from skeletal muscle using the PCT Shredder alone or with subsequent pressure cycling at 10,000 psi are generally intact and exhibit a range of morphologies similar to control samples.
14. The purity of the PCT Shredder and Shredder/pressure cycling muscle mitochondria samples is comparable to controls, containing a small proportion of non-mitochondria organelles and fragments.
15. Assess the purity of the skeletal muscle and kidney mitochondria preparations using proteomic approaches, comparing samples prepared by the Shredder/pressure cycling method to control samples prepared by a manual homogenizer.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation using the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling. The steps include tissue disruption using the PCT Shredder, differential centrifugation, and pressure cycling at different psi levels. The protocol also includes assays to assess mitochondrial yield, respiration, and response to Ca2+ overload. However, some protocol details are missing, such as the specific centrifugation speeds and durations, as well as the specific buffer compositions used.

summary:
Revised Summary: 
Centrifugation and pressure cycling-enhanced extraction, specifically using the PCT Shredder, were employed to isolate mitochondria from muscle tissue. This technique resulted in a higher yield and improved quality of the mitochondria compared to the traditional method. The pressure cycling process, which involved subjecting the crude homogenate to hydrostatic pressure, released additional mitochondria from cells. Respiration assays revealed that pressure cycling affected the respiration kinetics of the mitochondria, with normal kinetics observed at 10,000 psi and damage occurring at 30,000 psi. However, the ability of the muscle mitochondria to respond to Ca2+ overload was not significantly affected by pressure cycling. Morphological analysis using transmission electron microscopy confirmed that the mitochondria prepared using the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling exhibited intact morphology and high purity. This was further supported by proteomic analysis, which showed that the muscle mitochondria prepared by the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling method were indistinguishable from those prepared by manual homogenization. It is worth noting that the same high purity and intact morphology were observed in kidney mitochondria prepared by pressure cycling at 10,000 psi. Overall, the results demonstrate the effectiveness of the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling technique in isolating functionally active and high-quality mitochondria from muscle tissue. Visual representations of the experimental results can be found in Figures 2, 3, 4, and 5 in reference [25].

Extraction of high-quality functional mitochondria from various tissues is required for
studies that focus on mitochondrial function and dysfunction, as well as the role of
mitochondria as both targets for drug development and as off-target mediators of drug side
effects [26] New robust and reproducible methods for the isolation of intact and functional
mitochondria from a wide array of tissues can contribute to areas of research such as
cytotoxicity assays, discovery of potential mitochondria-targeted therapeutic agents, the
elucidation of signaling pathways, and in some cases, the diagnosis of certain disorders, as
well as mitochondrial proteomics, lipidomics and metabolomics The study of mitochondrial
phospholipids is also an emerging field that is likely to lead to greater understanding of
mitochondrial function in different tissues [27] Studies of tissue mitochondria have
demonstrated that the mitochondrial proteome is a dynamic system regulated by both theGross et al Page 11
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptnucleus and the mitochondrion itself For this reason, the mitochondrial proteomes of

significantly [28], highlighting the need for convenient mitochondria isolation methods can

sometimes contradictory, findings These inconsistent results are likely due, at least in part,
to differences in the quality of mitochondria as a result of different isolation procedures
([29]) or the experience of the operators Here we present simplified methods for extraction
of muscle tissue mitochondria Several parameters of the new method were also tested in
kidney mitochondria to demonstrate that the methods are likely to be generally applicable to
mitochondria from other tissues The key steps when isolating mitochondria from any tissue
are usually the same: (i) Tissue disruption using either mechanical methods alone, or a
combination of chemical and mechanical methods and (ii) subsequent differential
centrifugation to remove debris and large cellular organelles in order to enrich for
mitochondria Despite the importance of the tissue disruption step, there are few techniques
available to circumvent the many known drawbacks of homogenizer-based mechanical
tissue disruption for mitochondrial isolation Therefore, current methods require extensive
operator involvement in the tissue mincing and homogenization steps The homogenization
process is especially labor intensive when the tissue is fibrous like muscle The PCT
Shredder PULSE Tube simplifies the tissue disruption process Once the shredded tissue
homogenate is forced through the holes of the lysis disk, further grinding is unnecessary In
this way, the PCT Shredder provides both more rapid homogenization than is possible with
the Dounce or Potter-Elvehjem homogenizers, and prevents over-homogenization, such as
can easily occur with rotor/stator-type homogenizers, which can lead to rapid loss of
mitochondrial integrity Regardless of how tissue homogenization is performed, it is, at its core a stochastic process
and does not guarantee even distribution of mechanical force to the entire sample In
contrast, sample disruption by pressure cycling is a non-stochastic process in which pressure
is propagated uniformly throughout the sample and does not depend on any statistical
probability of a given cell coming in contact with any other cell or homogenizing surface Identical, non-shearing disruptive physical forces are exerted upon every cell in the sample,

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Spot densitometry image analysis carried out on the kidney mitochondria 2D gels using REDFIN 3 software confirmed no significant difference in protein pattern between samples isolated by different methods.
2. Western blotting confirmed that mitochondrial preparations from both skeletal muscle and kidney tissues were enriched in markers for all three mitochondrial compartments: voltage dependent anion channel (VDAC/porin), an outer mitochondrial membrane marker; prohibitin, a marker for the inner mitochondrial membrane; and HSP-60, a soluble protein localized to the mitochondrial inner matrix space.
3. SDS-PAGE gels demonstrated that the protein profiles of control mitochondria samples isolated by homogenizer were essentially the same as the test samples prepared using the PCT Shredder with pressure cycling for muscle and pressure cycling alone for kidney tissue.
4. The protein pattern in the mitochondria-enriched preparations differed from that seen with whole tissue lysate, which was not enriched in mitochondria.
5. Extraction of high-quality functional mitochondria from various tissues is required for studies focusing on mitochondrial function and dysfunction, drug development, drug side effects, cytotoxicity assays, discovery of potential mitochondria-targeted therapeutic agents, elucidation of signaling pathways, diagnosis of certain disorders, mitochondrial proteomics, lipidomics, metabolomics, and the study of mitochondrial phospholipids.
6. The mitochondrial proteome is a dynamic system regulated by both the nucleus and the mitochondrion itself, which can vary significantly between different tissues and disease states.
7. Simplified methods for extracting muscle tissue mitochondria were presented, with some parameters tested in kidney mitochondria to demonstrate general applicability to mitochondria from other tissues.
8. The key steps when isolating mitochondria from any tissue are tissue disruption using mechanical methods alone or a combination of chemical and mechanical methods, and subsequent differential centrifugation to remove debris and large cellular organelles to enrich for mitochondria.
9. Homogenizer-based mechanical tissue disruption for mitochondrial isolation has known drawbacks, and current methods require extensive operator involvement in tissue mincing and homogenization steps.
10. The PCT Shredder PULSE Tube simplifies the tissue disruption process, providing more rapid homogenization than Dounce or Potter-Elvehjem homogenizers, and preventing over-homogenization that can lead to rapid loss of mitochondrial integrity.
11. Sample disruption by pressure cycling is a non-stochastic process that exerts identical, non-shearing disruptive physical forces upon every cell in the sample, resulting in well-controlled and reproducible cell lysis.
12. Pressure cycling has been shown to be relatively gentle and has been used to isolate mitochondria from cell culture.

The article provides information on the isolation of mitochondria from skeletal muscle and kidney tissues. summary: Revised Summary: Researchers at Ludesi AB in Lund conducted a study on mitochondrial proteomics and phospholipids using REDFIN 3 software. They analyzed protein patterns in mitochondria-enriched samples from skeletal muscle and kidney tissues using Western blotting and SDS-PAGE gels. The study aimed to understand mitochondrial function and dysfunction, develop mitochondria-targeted therapeutic agents, investigate signaling pathways, and diagnose certain disorders through mitochondrial proteomics. The presence of Voltage dependent anion channel (VDAC/porin), prohibitin, and HSP-60 in the mitochondria-enriched samples confirmed their intactness. The researchers compared different methods for mitochondria isolation, including tissue disruption using mechanical methods and a combination of chemical and mechanical approaches. They emphasized the importance of well-controlled and reproducible cell lysis for gentle isolation of mitochondria from cell culture. The findings provide valuable insights for drug development, understanding drug side effects, conducting cytotoxicity assays, and exploring the role of mitochondria in cellular processes. The study also highlighted the use of the PCT Shredder device for tissue disruption and the pressure cycling technique for sample disruption and cell lysis. Kidney mitochondria and skeletal muscle were specifically studied in this research, and the researchers mentioned that the mitochondrial proteome can vary significantly between different tissues and disease states. The research paper includes Figure 6 and supplemental material for additional information, and references [26], [27], [28], [29], and [30] are cited.

Furthermore, pressure cycling in the Barocycler is likely to be more robust than
conventional extraction techniques in inexperienced hands because the process is automated
and programmable, relying less on operator experience and giving the user fine-tunable
control of cell disruption for extraction of intact and functional mitochondria with less
expected person-to-person and prep-to-prep variability Our results demonstrate that tissue disruption with the PCT Shredder, either alone or in
conjunction with pressure cycling, can be used to extract intact and functional mitochondria
from tissues such as rat skeletal muscle and kidney The potential to add pressure cycling to
isolation techniques may be useful for sample cleanup and preparation for biochemical
analysis Pressure cycling at 10,000 psi for five cycles at 4°C, does not result in any
measurable muscle or kidney mitochondria dysfunction, as assessed by respiration assays,
Pressure cycling at 20,000 psi can lead to slight reduction in RCR values in kidney
mitochondria (Table 2) but appears to have almost no negative impact on muscle
mitochondria (Figure 2), indicating that these tissue mitochondria can withstand pressures in
the 10,000 to 20,000 psi range Although it was found that pressure cycling at 10,000 psi
was sufficient to extract skeletal muscle mitochondria, it is likely that extraction from
different tissues may require different pressure cycling parameters The observation that fiveGross et al Page 12
Anal Biochem  Author manuscript; available in PMC 2012 November 15 NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscript NIH-PA Author Manuscriptcycles at 20,000 psi, still yields functional mitochondria, suggests that this method can be
used to extract high quality, intact mitochondria even from samples that may require harsher
conditions for cell lysis Conclusions
The data presented here confirm that mitochondria extracted using pressure cycling, or The
PCT Shredder, or a combination of both methods, are intact and functionally active, and can
be used for downstream applications that require high quality, enriched functional
mitochondria preparations These mitochondria exhibit respiration and calcium handling
properties that are comparable to controls isolated using standard methods performed by
skilled operators Muscle tissue disruption with The PCT Shredder, with or without
subsequent pressure cycling, is far less dependent on operator experience than traditional
manual homogenizer-based methods In addition, since pressure cycling in the Barocycler is
a programmable and hands-off procedure, it too facilitates reproducible, user-independent
mitochondria extraction from tissues The new methods require little hands-on tissue
homogenization, can be easily learned by a novice, and are expected to lead to results that
are more consistent and reproducible from lab to lab Because it is less reliant on operator
skill, the technique is also more amenable to scale-up Furthermore, for applications where
highly purified mitochondria are required, the mitochondria-enriched samples generated as
described above can be subjected to density gradient centrifugation, or other downstream
purification techniques Supplementary Material

summary: Summary 5: Pressure cycling in the Barocycler, an automated and programmable process, allows for fine-tunable control of cell disruption during extraction of intact and functional mitochondria. This method is more robust and less variable compared to conventional techniques, making it suitable for inexperienced users. The PCT Shredder, in combination with pressure cycling, effectively extracts intact and functional mitochondria from tissues like rat skeletal muscle and kidney. Pressure cycling at 10,000 psi for five cycles at 4°C does not cause muscle or kidney mitochondria dysfunction. Although pressure cycling at 20,000 psi slightly reduces RCR values in kidney mitochondria, it has almost no negative impact on muscle mitochondria. The use of pressure cycling can be extended to sample cleanup and preparation for biochemical analysis. Different tissues may require different pressure cycling parameters for extraction. The extraction methods using pressure cycling or the PCT Shredder, or a combination of both, yield intact and functionally active mitochondria that can be used for downstream applications. These mitochondria exhibit properties comparable to controls isolated using standard methods performed by skilled operators. The PCT Shredder, with or without pressure cycling, is less dependent on operator experience compared to traditional manual homogenizer-based methods. Pressure cycling in the Barocycler is a programmable and hands-off procedure, facilitating reproducible and user-independent mitochondria extraction. The new methods require minimal hands-on tissue homogenization and can be easily learned by novices. They are expected to lead to consistent and reproducible results across different laboratories. The technique is also amenable to scale-up and is suitable for applications requiring highly purified mitochondria. High-quality mitochondria obtained through density gradient centrifugation and other downstream purification techniques can be used to assess respiration assays and calcium handling properties, ensuring accurate measurements and consistent results. The work was supported by NIH SBIR grant GM079059 and the Genes Environment Initiative (PI, Kristal). The study involved personnel from Pressure BioSciences, including V Gross, A Lazarev, and G Carlson. Additionally, PubMed Central and BWH/Partners supported the study.

summary: Summary 4: This study explores the biochemical properties of subsarcolemmal and interfibrillar mitochondria in rat cardiac muscle. It also investigates the effects of reactive gamma-ketoaldehydes on mitochondrial respiration and calcium homeostasis. The study finds that endurance training can attenuate bioenergetics alterations in skeletal muscle mitochondria under acute hypoxia. Additionally, it reveals the key role of mitochondria in microcystin-LR kidney intoxication and the activation of mitochondrial permeability transition by antidiabetic sulphonylureas in skeletal muscle. The study highlights the importance of measuring mitochondrial respiratory control using ADP:O ratios and the significance of the respiratory chain and oxidative phosphorylation in mitochondria. It also presents a kinetic model for Ca2+-induced permeability transition in liver mitochondria and compares different fluorescent stains for protein differential staining in two-dimensional gels. Furthermore, the study discusses the biochemical differences between intermyofibrillar and subsarcolemmal mitochondria in human muscle and the response of mitochondrial fusion and fission protein gene expression to exercise in rat skeletal muscle. It explores the regulation of mitochondrial respiration by inorganic phosphate and the discovery of a novel potassium channel in skeletal muscle mitochondria. The study demonstrates the bioenergetic alterations and mitochondrial dysfunction in aging skeletal muscle and the effects of veratrine on ultrastructural and cytochemical changes in skeletal muscle mitochondria. It emphasizes the regulation of the permeability transition pore in skeletal muscle mitochondria and its significance in mitochondrial toxicity testing and drug development. The study also compares the subsarcolemmal mitochondrial membrane phospholipid fatty acid composition in different types of rat skeletal muscle and discusses the dynamic functional program of the mitochondrial proteome in tissues and disease states. Furthermore, it highlights the role of mitochondria and free radicals in the biology of aging and the isolation of mitochondria from cell cultures using pressure cycling technology for proteomic analysis. The study concludes that the PCT Shredder and pressure cycling can be used for tissue disruption to extract mitochondria for analysis, and respiratory control and ADP:O ratios are important in assessing mitochondrial respiratory function.

summary: **Summary 4**: This study by Gross et al. investigates the impact of pressure cycling and the PCT Shredder on mitochondrial function, protein expression, and morphology in skeletal muscle and kidney. Respiration assays using complex I and complex II substrates reveal the influence of substrate utilization on respiratory parameters. Mitochondrial function and ATP production can be assessed by measuring respiratory parameters and substrate utilization. The PCT Shredder, in combination with pressure cycling, is effective for isolating mitochondria from skeletal muscle and kidney. Protein profiling provides insights into protein expression and mitochondrial function. SDS-PAGE and Western blotting techniques can be used to evaluate mitochondrial swelling. The study highlights the effects of pressure cycling and the PCT Shredder on respiratory parameters and explores their impact on mitochondrial morphology and energy metabolism.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Extracted Steps:**

1. Respiratory control ratios of kidney mitochondria were measured using either the longer original protocol or the shorter simplified method.
2. Respiration was measured using complex 1 substrate.
3. The original method involved the isolation of kidney mitochondria (n=4).
4. The simplified method involved the isolation of kidney mitochondria (n=8).
5. The effect of KH 2PO4 concentration on muscle mitochondria respiratory parameters in the presence of glutamate-malate was measured.
6. Muscle mitochondria were prepared using 1 mM PO4 (n=3) or 10 mM PO4 (n=3).
7. The optimized respiration buffer for rat skeletal muscle mitochondria was determined.
8. The respiration buffer contained 25mM HEPES, 1mM KH 2PO 4, 50μM EDTA, 1mM MgCl 2, 125mM KCl, 10mM NaCl, pH 7.2 (n=3).
9. Another respiration buffer was tested, containing 25mM HEPES, 10mM KH 2PO 4, 25μM EGTA, 0mM MgCl 2, 125mM KCl, 10mM NaCl, pH 7.2 (n=2).
10. The influence of g-force on the yield of skeletal muscle mitochondrial protein was investigated.
11. Skeletal muscle mitochondria were extracted at 10,000 psi for 5 cycles and prepared using 7,500 × g, 10,000 × g, or 14,000 × g for the first high-speed spin.
12. Subsequent spins were all conducted at 14,000 × g.
13. The yield of mitochondria enriched pellet recovered per gram starting tissue mass was measured (7,500 × g: n=3, 10,000 × g: n=4, 14,000 × g: n=18).
14. The influence of g-force on the respiration of skeletal muscle mitochondria was studied.
15. Skeletal muscle mitochondria were extracted at 10,000 psi for 5 cycles and prepared using 14,000 × g or 7,500 × g for the first high-speed spin.
16. Subsequent spins were all conducted at 14,000 × g.
17. Respiration was measured using complex I substrate (7,500 × g: n=3, 14,000 × g: n=7).
18. The calcium retention capacity of mitochondria isolated from skeletal muscle using a homogenizer or The PCT Shredder with pressure cycling at 10,000 psi was determined.
19. Mitochondria were energized with succinate alone (5mM), succinate with 1 μM rotenone, or succinate with rotenone and 1 μM cyclosporin A (CsA).
20. Calcium retention capacity (nmol/mg of protein) was measured by calculating the total amount of Ca2+ used to induce mPT.
21. Values were obtained for skeletal muscle mitochondria prepared using a homogenizer (SM Homog) and The PCT Shredder (SM Shredder), as well as rat liver mitochondria (RLM) prepared using a conventional homogenizer method.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide the detailed steps for the original or simplified protocols for kidney mitochondria isolation.
- The article does not provide the detailed steps for muscle mitochondria isolation in the presence of glutamate-malate.
- The article does not provide the detailed steps for the extraction of skeletal muscle mitochondria using 10,000 psi for 5 cycles.
- The article does not provide the detailed steps for the respiration measurement using complex I

- Kidney mitochondria
- Muscle mitochondria
- Rat skeletal muscle mitochondria
- Rat liver mitochondria

summary:
Summary 4:
1. Kidney mitochondria can be prepared using either the longer original protocol or a simplified method. Respiratory control ratios were measured using complex 1 substrate, and the results are presented in Table 3.
2. The effect of KH 2PO4 concentration on muscle mitochondria respiratory parameters was examined, and the results are presented in Table 4.
3. An optimized respiration buffer for rat skeletal muscle mitochondria was determined, and the composition is presented in Table 5.
4. The yield of skeletal muscle mitochondrial protein can be influenced by the g-force used during the isolation process, as shown in Table 6.
5. The respiration of skeletal muscle mitochondria can also be affected by the g-force applied during isolation, as shown in Table 7.
6. The calcium retention capacity of skeletal muscle mitochondria can be determined using either a homogenizer or the PCT Shredder with pressure cycling, as shown in Table 8. Comparison with rat liver mitochondria is also presented.
7. The simplified method for isolating muscle mitochondria from rat skeletal muscle using glutamate-malate as the complex 1 substrate was evaluated.
8. Respiration was measured using ADP/O in a respiration buffer containing HEPES, EDTA, MgCl2, KCl, and NaCl.
9. Protein isolation from muscle mitochondria was performed using centrifugation, and the yield was determined.
10. The respiration and calcium retention capacity in skeletal muscle mitochondria were assessed.
11. A comparison of calcium retention capacity between skeletal muscle mitochondria and rat liver mitochondria was made.
12. The respiration buffer for rat skeletal muscle mitochondria was optimized.
13. The influence of g-force on the yield of skeletal muscle mitochondrial protein was investigated.
14. The influence of g-force on respiration in skeletal muscle mitochondria was examined.
15. The study focused on mitochondrial function, biochemical analysis, protein isolation, and mitochondrial quality.
16. Calcium regulation and tissue specificity in mitochondria were also analyzed.
